questions.question: As a general rule in Irish criminal law, a person cannot be convicted of a criminal offence for failing to act. However, there are recognised exceptions where an omission can ground criminal liability. Which of the following is NOT a recognised basis for imposing criminal liability for an omission?

options.B: A contractual duty where the contract is for the protection of a class of persons
options.C: A moral duty arising from the defendant's knowledge of the danger
options.D: A duty arising from the defendant's prior dangerous act creating risk

questions.explanation: Irish and English criminal law recognise several categories of duty that can give rise to liability for omissions: (1) statutory duties (e.g., road traffic obligations); (2) contractual duties where the contract exists for the protection of persons (R v Pittwood [1902] — a railway gatekeeper's contractual duty ran to users of the crossing); (3) duties arising from a special relationship (R v Gibbins and Proctor — parent's duty to child); (4) duties arising from the defendant's voluntary assumption of responsibility (R v Stone and Dobinson [1977]); (5) duties arising from the defendant's prior dangerous act (R v Miller [1983]). A mere moral duty — arising simply from knowing that someone is in danger — has never been accepted as a basis for criminal liability for omission in Irish or English law. This is the fundamental distinction between moral and legal obligation.

questions.question: In R v Miller [1983], Miller (a squatter) accidentally started a fire by dropping a cigarette on a mattress. He woke up, saw the fire, and simply moved to another room without taking any steps to extinguish the fire or raise the alarm. The house was damaged. The House of Lords convicted him of arson. On what basis was liability founded?

options.A: Miller had a contractual duty as a squatter to maintain the property
options.B: Miller had a special relationship with the owner of the property
options.C: Miller's prior dangerous act (accidentally starting the fire) created a duty on him to take reasonable steps to avert the danger he had created when he became aware of it
options.D: Miller had assumed responsibility for the property by moving in

questions.explanation: R v Miller [1983] is the leading authority establishing that where a defendant inadvertently creates a dangerous situation, he comes under a duty to take reasonable steps to avert the danger once he becomes aware of it. The House of Lords held that the actus reus of arson was constituted not merely by the initial act of dropping the cigarette, but by the continuing state of affairs which Miller allowed to persist when he failed to act on becoming aware of the fire. Lord Diplock articulated the 'duty arising from prior dangerous act' principle. This is a crucial exception to the general 'no liability for omission' rule. Option A is incorrect — squatters have no such contractual duty. Option B is incorrect — there was no special relationship with the property owner. Option D (assumed responsibility) relates to a different exception (R v Stone and Dobinson).

questions.question: Mary is a qualified nurse who agrees, as a favour to a friend, to care for an elderly relative who is ill. Mary performs this care for three weeks but then abandons the care without arranging any alternative. The elderly relative dies. On what basis might Mary be held criminally liable for the death?

options.A: Mary had a statutory duty of care as a nurse
options.B: Mary had voluntarily assumed responsibility for the care of the elderly relative, creating a duty she could not simply abandon without taking steps to ensure the relative's safety
options.C: Mary had a duty arising from prior dangerous act — her care had created a dependence
options.D: Mary is not liable because she was acting gratuitously and had no legal obligation to continue

questions.explanation: This scenario closely mirrors R v Stone and Dobinson [1977], where the defendants had voluntarily assumed responsibility for caring for a vulnerable person. Once a person voluntarily assumes responsibility for the welfare of another who is dependent on them, they come under a legal duty to continue that care or to make safe arrangements for the person's welfare before withdrawing. Mary's voluntary assumption of the care role, the elderly relative's resulting dependence, and the abrupt abandonment without alternative arrangements all point to liability through the 'voluntary assumption of responsibility' exception to the omissions rule. Option A incorrectly assumes her nursing qualification creates a standalone statutory duty in this context. Option C (Miller principle) relates to inadvertently created danger, not assumed care. Option D is wrong — the gratuitous nature of the assumption does not defeat the duty once dependence has been created.

questions.question: Section 176 of the Criminal Justice Act 2006 in Ireland creates an offence of failing to report child pornography to the Gardaí. This is an example of which basis for criminal liability through omission?
questions.explanation: Section 176 of the Criminal Justice Act 2006 imposes a statutory duty on persons who become aware of child pornography to report it to the Gardaí. Failure to comply with this statutory reporting obligation constitutes a criminal offence, making this a paradigm example of criminal liability arising from a statutory duty to act. The Irish Oireachtas has similarly created mandatory reporting obligations in child protection legislation (Children First Act 2015) and other areas. Statutory duties represent the clearest and most straightforward basis for imposing criminal liability for omissions because the legislature has expressly identified both the duty and the consequences of failure. Options A, B, and D describe common law exceptions developed by the courts, not the statutory basis illustrated here.

questions.question: A passerby (who has no special relationship with anyone involved) witnesses a child drowning in a shallow pool. The passerby could easily save the child with no risk to themselves but walks on. The child dies. In Irish criminal law, is the passerby criminally liable for the child's death?

options.A: Yes — the passerby had a moral duty to act which the law treats as equivalent to a legal duty
options.B: Yes — because the passerby had the capacity to save the child without risk to themselves
options.C: No — absent a recognised legal duty (special relationship, statutory duty, contractual duty, assumed responsibility, or prior dangerous act), a failure to rescue does not ground criminal liability in Irish law
options.D: Yes — the proximity of the passerby to the danger creates a legal duty to act

questions.explanation: This is the classic 'stranger drowning' scenario that illustrates the fundamental common law position: there is no general duty to rescue. In the absence of one of the recognised categories of legal duty — special relationship, statutory duty, contractual duty, voluntarily assumed responsibility, or duty arising from prior dangerous act — a bystander who fails to act incurs no criminal liability, however morally reprehensible their inaction may be. Irish law has not adopted a general duty to rescue (unlike some European jurisdictions such as France and Germany, which impose statutory good samaritan obligations). Options A and D articulate moral rather than legal duties. Option B (capacity without risk) might support a moral argument for liability but is not a recognised legal duty in Irish criminal law.

questions.question: In R v Pittwood [1902], a railway gatekeeper failed to close the level crossing gate before a train arrived. A hay cart crossed the line and was struck by the train, killing the carter. Pittwood was convicted of manslaughter. Which duty category grounded his liability?

options.A: Special relationship — Pittwood had a duty to all road users as a public servant
options.B: Contractual duty — Pittwood's employment contract required him to keep the gate closed; the contract was for the protection of road users
options.C: Assumed responsibility — Pittwood had voluntarily taken on the role of gatekeeper
options.D: Statutory duty — railway safety legislation imposed a duty on gatekeepers

questions.explanation: In R v Pittwood [1902], Wright J held that Pittwood's criminal liability arose from his contractual duty. Pittwood was employed as a gatekeeper under a contract that required him to close the gate before trains passed. Crucially, the contract was for the protection of road users who relied on the gate being closed. This extended the contractual duty beyond the parties to the contract (Pittwood and the railway company) to encompass third-party road users who were the intended beneficiaries of the safety obligation. This case established that a contractual duty, where it exists for the protection of a class of persons, can ground criminal liability for an omission. Option A is incorrect — Pittwood was not a 'public servant' in the relevant sense. Option C (voluntary assumption) overlaps with contractual duty but is a distinct category. Option D is incorrect on the facts; no statute was specifically relied upon.

questions.question: In Fagan v Metropolitan Police Commissioner [1969], Fagan accidentally drove onto a police officer's foot. When told to remove the car, he deliberately refused for a period before eventually complying. The car remained on the officer's foot. The court convicted Fagan of assault. How did the court conceptualise the actus reus?

options.A: The initial driving onto the foot was an assault with sufficient mens rea
options.B: The deliberate refusal to move was an omission that was itself the assault
options.C: The act of driving onto the foot and the continued application of force constituted a continuing act: the actus reus was ongoing, and mens rea could attach at any point during the continuing act
options.D: Fagan had assumed responsibility for the officer's wellbeing by the initial act

questions.explanation: In Fagan v Metropolitan Police Commissioner [1969], the Divisional Court developed the 'continuing act' doctrine to address the problem that at the moment of initial contact (driving onto the foot), Fagan lacked mens rea (it was accidental). The court held that the application of force was not a single instantaneous act but a continuing one: so long as the car remained on the officer's foot, the actus reus was ongoing. Once Fagan deliberately refused to move (thereby forming the requisite mens rea), both actus reus and mens rea coexisted at the same time. The continuing act doctrine bridges the requirement for coincidence of actus reus and mens rea in situations of initial inadvertent contact followed by deliberate maintenance of force. This case is also relevant to the coincidence rule. Options A, B, and D mischaracterise the basis of liability.

questions.question: Under the Children First Act 2015, certain 'mandated persons' in Ireland (such as teachers, social workers, and healthcare professionals) are required to report concerns about child welfare to Tusla. Failure to report constitutes an offence. A teacher who suspects abuse but fails to report would be liable on which basis?

options.B: Special relationship — as a teacher, she has a special relationship with her pupils
options.C: Statutory duty to act — the Children First Act 2015 imposes a mandatory reporting obligation on mandated persons
options.D: Contractual duty — her employment contract requires professional conduct

questions.explanation: The Children First Act 2015 is a prime example of the Irish legislature imposing statutory duties to act as a tool of child protection policy. 'Mandated persons' (a defined category including teachers, social workers, doctors, nurses, and others who work with children in a professional capacity) are legally required to report known or reasonably suspected child abuse or neglect to Tusla. Failure to comply is an offence. This is unambiguously a statutory duty to act. While Option B (special relationship) and Option D (contractual duty) might have some independent merit, the specific statutory basis is what the Children First Act establishes and what grounds criminal liability in this context. Option A (voluntary assumption of responsibility) applies to situations not governed by statute.

questions.question: A parent fails to feed their young child over several weeks, resulting in the child's death from starvation. The parent argues they had no intention to kill and simply did not act. Which is the most accurate statement of the criminal law applicable to this scenario?

options.A: The parent cannot be convicted of any offence because the death resulted from an omission, not a positive act
options.B: The parent may be convicted of murder or manslaughter because parents have a legal duty arising from a special relationship to provide for their children's basic needs, and a deliberate failure to discharge that duty can found liability for homicide
options.C: The parent may only be convicted of the offence of wilful neglect under child welfare legislation, not murder or manslaughter
options.D: The parent is liable only if they knew the child would die from starvation

questions.explanation: Parents have a well-established special relationship with their children that imposes a legal duty to provide the necessities of life including food, shelter, clothing, and medical attention. This duty exists both at common law and is reinforced by statute (Children Act 2001 in Ireland). A deliberate failure by a parent to provide food to a young child who is entirely dependent on them for sustenance can constitute the actus reus of homicide, with liability for murder or manslaughter depending on the parent's mens rea. In R v Gibbins and Proctor (1918), a father and his partner who deliberately starved the father's daughter were convicted of murder. Option A is incorrect — the law recognises duties arising from special relationships. Option C understates the potential liability — murder or manslaughter are available charges. Option D is incorrect — for manslaughter the mental element is lower than knowledge of certain death.
